policy_statement: Enable community resiliency by resourcing community-led climate infrastructure projects and integrating resiliency strategies into hazard mitigation planning.
mechanism_description: Resource community-led resilience infrastructure and integrate resilience into hazard mitigation planning to improve institutional and community capacity to anticipate, withstand, and recover from climate hazards.
primary_causal_pathway: Governance/planning capacity building → improved preparedness/coordination for climate hazards → ↓ vulnerability/exposure/impacts
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Governments and communities adopt structured adaptation planning frameworks (hazard mitigation integration, coverage targets, indicators, monitoring and evaluation, resourcing community-led resilience projects). (2) Mediating process: Improved risk assessment, prioritization, cross-agency coordination, funding/program delivery, and learning/iteration increases the ability to anticipate and manage hazards (institutional adaptive capacity). (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced damages and service disruptions from climate extremes and slow-onset changes via better preparedness, response, and recovery.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced climate-risk impacts (lower vulnerability/exposure and faster recovery) through stronger institutional and community preparedness and coordination.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: planning/targets/resourcing → stronger institutions/community systems → reduced climate hazard impacts.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: the mechanism’s direct objective is risk management capacity (preparedness/coordination), not primarily GHG reduction (Mitigation), reduced resource input for the same service (Resource Efficiency), or ecosystem-mediated benefits (Nature-Based Solutions).  
3) Inherent causal chain for each secondary: none—any mitigation/resource-efficiency co-benefits depend on what specific projects are chosen under the planning framework and are not guaranteed by “planning capacity” itself.  
4) Edge cases applied: even if some plans include mitigation actions, the canonical mechanism here is the planning/institutional capacity pathway; emissions outcomes are optional and downstream of separate mechanisms.  
5) Confirm labels apply to ALL instances: any policy that expands adaptation planning coverage, integrates resilience into hazard planning, or sets adaptation plan indicators increases adaptive capacity and thus is Adaptation in all contexts.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the “planning framework” mandates specific emissions-reducing measures (e.g., required building electrification) as the central action, that would shift to Mitigation for that policy; similarly, if the framework exclusively drives nature-based implementations (e.g., mandated urban canopy expansion), NBS could become primary. Under the stated mechanism (institutional adaptive capacity), Adaptation remains primary.
key_indicators: "Enable community resiliency"; "resourcing community-led climate infrastructure projects"; "integrating resiliency strategies into hazard mitigation planning"; "STRATEGY 3"
co_benefits: Potential co-benefits: improved social cohesion/community capacity; possible economic development/job creation from local infrastructure projects; improved public safety/disaster response generally.
instance_edge_case_notes: The verbatim text is only a strategy header and does not specify the actual instruments (e.g., grants, mandates, funding levels). Classification as a planning strategy is therefore based primarily on the policy_statement rather than detailed source language.
